title: HSV: Separating Color from Brightness
concept: HSV describes a color with Hue (which color it is), Saturation (how vivid vs. washed-out), and Value (how bright vs. dark) — a way of organizing color that matches how humans actually describe it, and makes it easier to isolate a specific color regardless of lighting.

exercises.prompt: If someone describes a color to you, are they more likely to say 'R=200, G=40, B=40' or 'a dark, dull red'?

options.text: A dark, dull red — people describe color by hue and brightness, not by channel numbers.
R=200, G=40, B=40 — people naturally think in RGB triplets.
Neither — people never describe colors in words.

exercises.explain: Right — 'dark, dull red' is exactly hue + saturation + value in plain language.

cards.title: RGB numbers don't match how you talk about color
cards.body: If a friend asks what color your shirt is, you say 'a bright red' or 'a pale blue,' not 'R=220, G=20, B=30.' RGB is great for lighting up pixels, but it isn't how humans naturally think about color.

cards.title: Hue, Saturation, Value
cards.body: HSV represents a color with three different measurements: Hue is which color it is (red, green, blue, yellow...), placed around a 0-360° color wheel. Saturation is how vivid or washed-out that color is, from gray to fully intense. Value is how bright or dark it is, from black up to full brightness.

cards.title: Pick a crayon, then dilute it, then dim the lights
cards.body: Think of choosing a crayon color first — that's your Hue. Then imagine mixing in some gray to fade it — that's Saturation dropping. Then imagine dimming the room lights over it — that's Value dropping. Three separate knobs, each doing one clear job, instead of three tangled RGB numbers.

cards.title: Why HSV is great for finding 'the red thing'
cards.body: Say you want a program to spot a red ball in a photo. In sunlight the ball might be R=230,G=20,B=15; in shade it might be R=110,G=8,B=6 — completely different RGB numbers for the same red object. In HSV, both versions share almost the same Hue — only the Value changes with the lighting. Searching for 'Hue near red' is far more reliable than chasing RGB triplets around.

cards.title: HSV isn't a different set of colors
cards.body: It's easy to assume HSV and RGB show different colors, since the numbers look nothing alike. They don't — every color an RGB pixel can hold has a matching Hue, Saturation, and Value. HSV is the same underlying colors, just re-organized to answer the question 'what color, how vivid, how bright' instead of 'how much red, green, blue.'

cards.title: Two shapes for the same colors
cards.body: RGB and HSV organize identical colors around different structures.

left.text:
Three axes: red, green, blue.
A color is a point in 3D space.
Hard to say 'just change the color, not the brightness.'

right.text:
Spin around for Hue.
Move outward for Saturation.
Move up for Value.
Easy to isolate one property at a time.

exercises.prompt: In HSV, which component tells you whether a color is vivid and rich versus grayish and dull?

options.text: None of them — HSV can't describe that.

exercises.explain: Correct — Saturation measures how vivid a color is; low saturation looks washed-out or gray.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Two photos of the same red object taken in bright light and in shadow will usually have very different Hue values.
exercises.explain: False — the Hue stays roughly the same in both lighting conditions; it's mainly the Value (brightness) that changes.

exercises.sentence: In HSV, the ___ describes how bright or dark a color is, separate from what color it is.
exercises.explain: Value is correct — it's the brightness dial, kept separate from which color (Hue) and how vivid (Saturation).

exercises.prompt: A developer says: 'I'm building a program to detect orange traffic cones under changing outdoor light, so I convert each frame to HSV and just filter by Hue.' Is that reasoning sound?

options.text: Yes — Hue stays fairly stable for a given color even as lighting brightness changes, unlike raw RGB values.
No — HSV can't represent orange, only primary colors.
No — Hue changes constantly with lighting, so this would fail immediately.

exercises.explain: Yes — that's precisely why HSV is a standard choice for lighting-robust color detection.

exercises.prompt: You're building a simple 'find the green traffic light' detector that has to work at noon and at dusk. Which approach best uses HSV's strengths?

options.text: Convert each frame to HSV and filter pixels by a Hue range near green, largely ignoring Value.
Stay in RGB and search for pixels where G is exactly 255.
Convert to HSV and filter by Value only, ignoring Hue.
Average the whole image to one RGB color and compare it to 'green'.

exercises.explain: Right — filtering on Hue (with a loose Saturation/Value range) is what makes HSV detection resilient to changing brightness.
